content.full_text:
February 10, 1977


Mr. James C. Dutra, Editor
VIEWPOINT
P.O. Box 8833
Stockton, CA 95208

Dear Mr. Dutra:

Your letter of December 26, 1976 to the Secretary
of State has been forwarded to the Fair Political Practices
Commission for a reply.

In your letter you describe the nature of the
publication VIEWPOINT and ask for advice regarding any report-
ing obligations which may result because of the publication.
You state the purpose of VIEWPOINT is to promote Republican
philosophy, aid local Republican organizations in promoting
their events, and promote candidates selected in the Republican
primaries. You also state that a subscription to VIEWPOINT
will cost $5.00 per year and the subscriber will be guaranteed
that $2.00 of the subscription cost will be used to defray the
costs of mailing an unsolicited special edition(s) of the
publication to voting households following the 1978 Republican
primaries. The remaining $3.00 will be used to pay costs of
publishing regular monthly issues.

Under the foregoing circumstances your organization
will become a "committee" within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act and incur reporting obligations when it receives
$50,0000 or more in payments which are "earmarked" for political
purposes. Based on the facts contained in your letter, the
money you receive which may be "earmarked" for political
purposes is the $2.00 portion of the subscription cost which
will be used for special editions of VIEWPOINT. Please note,
however, that the Political Reform Act does not require
disclosure of contributions or expenditures in connection with
federal campaigns and, consequently, the portion of the $2.00
used to promote federal candidates need not be considered in
determining whether your organization meets the $500.00
threshold level and incurs reporting obligations. Only the
portion of the $2.00 devoted to supporting state and local
candidates and ballot measures need be counted in determining
whether you reach the threshold. The $3.00 portion of the
subscription cost need not be counted because a subscriber will
receive full and adequate consideration for the $3.00.

Mr. James C. Dutra, Editor
February 10, 1977
Page Two


Enclosed, please find two regulations adopted by the
Commission (2 Cal. Adm. Codes Sections 18215 and 18225) which
you may find helpful for further clarification.

In your letter, you also ask whether or not there is
a limit on contributions which your organization may receive.

While the Political Reform Act requires disclosure
of contributions, there are no provisions which limit the
total amount of contributions.

Finally, you ask if the $2.00 that is set aside
in the subscription will be taxable.

For an answer to this last question, I suggest that
you contact:

Franchise Tax Board
1001 G Street, Suite 302
Sacramento, CA  95814
Phone: (916) 445-1426

I hope the above advice is helpful. If you expect
that your organization will incur reporting obligations or
if you have any other questions, please contact me for
further assistance.

Sincerely,

/s/
John C. Greenwood
Program Coordinator

JCG:ev

Attachments

VIEWPOINT
P.O. Box 8838
Stockton, Ca. 95208

December 25, 1976

California Secretary of State
Elections and Political Reform Division
111 Capitol Mall, Suite 200
Sacramento, Ca. 95814

Gentlemen:

I am hopeful that you will provide me with a
written definitive course of action for meeting any
political regulations that the enclosed publication
may fall under.

VIEWPOINT is a new publication which I am publishing
privately. Its first edition was printed and mailed on
December 22, 1976 free of charge.

Its stated purpose is to promote Republican philosophy
and to aid local Republican organizations in promoting their
events and to promote candidates selected in the Republican
primaries. But it makes no specific endorsement of a
candidate or political party.

It seeks a subscription of $5.00 per year for 12
monthly issues. It guarantees the subscriber that $2.00
of his subscription will be held to defray costs of mailing
an unsolicited special edition(s) of the publication to
voting households following the 1978 Republican primaries.
The remaining $3.00 will go towards publication costs of
the monthly issues.

Please advise as to the following:

1. As we will be receiving in excess of $500.00, do we need
to organize as a political committee?

2. If we need to organize as a political committee, can we
accept advertising which we plan to do from individuals,
companies, corporations, unions? What patronage would this
advertising revenue be, i.e., political donation, business
expense, etc?

3. Is there a contribution limit which one individual, company
corporation may provide the publication either in
subscriptions form, outright donation or other forms?

4. Will the $2.00 that is set aside in the subscription be
taxable at year's end?

Please advise as to any other regulations that may
apply.

James C. Dutra, Editor
